question: What are the roles at the table (players vs GM/referee/judge/MC), and what decisions does each role control?
answer: Players decide what their characters do, describing their actions and choices. The Referee decides and describes the setting and what characters perceive, decides what happens as a result of the characters’ actions, creates and runs the adventure (including monsters), and decides the results of player decisions for their characters. The Referee does not tell players what their characters do; instead the Referee referees the results according to the rules and interprets what the rules mean. The rules are presented as guidelines rather than a complete procedure for every possibility: there is not a rule for everything, and when in doubt, the Referee makes a ruling.
expected_answer_summary: Players choose and describe character actions; the Referee runs the world/adventure and adjudicates results, interpreting the rules and making rulings when needed.

gold_chunks.text: ![](images/0.jpg) — MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — READING THE MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — The following is a quick reference guide for how to read monster descriptions: — Hit Dice (HD) is the number of dice (d8) rolled to determine an individual creature’s hit points. If there is a plus or minus next to the number, you add or subtract that number from the total, only once. For example: for a creature with 4+1 hit dice, you would roll 4d8, and then add one more hit point. — Armor Class (AC) is explained in the rules for combat. If you are using the descending AC system (where a lower AC is better), disregard the numbers in brackets. If you are using the ascending system (where a high AC is better), use the numbers in brackets. — Attacks (Atk) includes the number of attacks the monster has, and the damage these attacks inflict if they hit. Monsters get a separate attack roll for each attack. Monster attack rolls are made on the Monster Attack Table (p. 38). Note: if you are using the “ascending AC system,” do not bother using the chart, because there is a quicker way: Roll to hit, add the monster’s hit dice, and if the result is equal to or greater than the character’s armor class, the attack hits. — Saving Throw (Save) means the target number (on a d20) the monster needs to meet or exceed in order to make a successful saving throw. — Morale (Morale) is an optional rule used to determine if monsters flee from combat or surrender. — Special (Special) is usually just a “flag” for the Referee, to remind him or her that the monster has some unusual ability or feature. If necessary, more explanation may be included in the monster description.

Move (Move) is the monster’s movement rate, and it is handled just as movement rates are handled for characters. — Percent in Lair (% in Lair) is used when a monster is encountered on a random table (such as the wilderness encounter tables). Roll 1d100, and if the result is equal to or lower than the number listed, the characters have stumbled upon the monster’s lair. For some monsters, the concept of a lair is largely meaningless (they don’t travel, don’t make a nest, or live on another plane of existence) and these might be either “0” or “100,” depending on what seems most appropriate. — Challenge Level (CL) is used to separate the monsters into “difficulty levels,” so when you are creating an adventure you have some guidelines about what the characters can handle and what they cannot. — XP (XP) tells how many experience points the adventuring party gains for killing the creature. In some cases, you may choose to award experience points for defeating a creature without killing it. Circumventing a monster by creative means, or capturing it to bring home, are two examples of when such an award might be made. — A short stat block after each monster description offers relevant details of the creature listed in a smaller form. It includes all of the information presented in the larger entry, including special attacks. These descriptions are often used in adventures to describe a monster quickly so you can keep playing without having to look them up.

![](images/0.jpg) — HOW TO PLAY — Once you have created a character, the Referee will describe the setting and what your character can see. The game might start in a rural peasant village, in a vast and teeming city spiked with towers and minarets, in a castle, in a tavern, or at the gates of an ancient tomb — that is up to the Referee. But from that point on, you describe what your character does. Going down stairs, attacking a dragon, talking to people on the street — all of these sorts of things are your decisions. The Referee tells you what happens as a result. Maybe those stairs lead down to a huge and echoing tomb, or perhaps that dragon attacks your character with a storm of fiery breath. These are the Referee’s decisions. The rules below are guidelines for how to handle certain events: combat, movement, healing, dying, and other important parts of the game. — Basically, you and the Referee work together, with the Referee handling the details of a dangerous fantasy world, and you deciding what your character does in it. The epic story of your character’s rise to greatness (or unfortunate death) is yours to create in the Referee’s world.

question: When the outcome is uncertain, what is the default way to resolve it (dice/cards/other), and how do you read the result (target number, degrees of success, opposing rolls, etc.)?
answer: The game resolves uncertainty using dice, with the specific die and success rule defined by the procedure you are using. The rules define standard polyhedral dice notation (d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, d20) and percentile dice (d100 as two d10s for tens and ones). For example, saving throws are resolved by rolling 1d20 and succeeding if the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number (which depends on class/level, and monsters have a listed target number). In combat, initiative is resolved by each side rolling 1d6; the higher result wins initiative for that round. Surprise (when applicable) is checked by rolling 1d6 for a side: on a 1–2 the group is surprised (with stated class-based exceptions).
expected_answer_summary: Uncertainty is resolved with dice; each procedure specifies what die to roll and how to read it (e.g., saves: d20 ≥ target; initiative: sides roll d6 high wins; surprise: d6 with 1–2 surprised).

gold_chunks.text: DICE — Swords & Wizardry uses several different kinds of dice, abbreviated according to how many sides they have. A four-sided die is called a d4, and if you roll 3 of them (adding the results together), that is written as 3d4. The six-sided die is a d6, the eight-sided die is a d8, the 10-sided die is a d10, the 12-sided die is a d12, and the 20-sided die is a d20. When the rules mention d100 (or percentile dice), roll two 10-sided dice, treating the first roll as the “tens” and the second roll as the “ones.” If you were to roll a 7 and then a 3, the result would be 73. A roll of 0 and 0 means a result of “100.”

SAVING THROWS — From time to time, a spell or some other kind of hazard requires you to make a “saving throw.” A successful saving throw means that the character (or monster) avoids the threat or lessens its effect. Each character class has a “Saving Throw” target number, which gets lower and lower — easier to beat, in other words — as a character gains levels. To make a saving throw, roll a d20. If the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number, the saving throw succeeds.
Take note: Some character classes have better-than-average chances to make a saving throw against particular types of hazards. Magic-Users, for example, get a bonus of +2 to the die roll on their saving throws against spells of all kinds. These various bonuses are described in the explanation of each character class. Fighters, Paladins, and Rangers do not get these bonuses because their base saving throw numbers are better than the other classes across the board.

INITIATIVE AND ORDER OF BATTLE — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Movement and Missile Fire: Each combatant on the side that won initiative may move, or may fire any missile weapons. Then the losers of the initiative roll may move or fire missile weapons. — 5. Melee Combat and Spells: Each combatant on the side that won initiative makes any melee attacks (if in melee range) or casts spells. Spells take effect immediately. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells.

6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished. — 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

question: How does time advance during play (turns/rounds/scenes/phases/ticks), and what causes the game to move from one step to the next?
answer: Time can advance in larger jumps when the Referee rules that hours or months pass, but the rules call out two key time units used for procedures: the turn and the combat round. A turn represents 10 minutes and is used to measure normal movement in dangerous places; a combat round represents 1 minute and is used to measure time while the party is in combat. The movement tables apply these units directly (walking/running speeds per turn, and combat movement per round). In combat, time advances round-by-round following an ordered sequence (check surprise if needed, declare spells, determine initiative, resolve movement/missile fire, resolve melee/spells, then the round ends and you repeat from declaring spells until combat ends). Special case: the Referee Guide describes a mass-combat procedure where combat rounds are longer (5 minutes for five-man units, 10 minutes for ten-man units), and initiative determines whether a side moves/attacks first or last within that larger-scale round.
expected_answer_summary: Procedural time uses turns (10 minutes) and combat rounds (1 minute) for movement/combat; combat advances by repeating a step-by-step round sequence; mass combat can use longer 5–10 minute rounds (special rule).

gold_chunks.text: TIME — Sometimes the Referee will rule that “an hour passes,” or even, “a month passes,” in the life of the intrepid adventurers. However, two important time measurements need quick explanations. These are the “turn,” and the “combat round.” A turn represents 10 minutes, and a combat round is 1 minute. — Turns are used to measure normal movement when the adventurers are in a dangerous place; combat rounds are used to measure time when the party is in combat. See the Movement Rate tables as an example of how “rounds” and “turns” are used.

Indoor, Underground, and City Movement — A turn represents 10 minutes, and a round represents 1 minute of combat. —
DescriptionSpeedResults
WalkingBase movement rate times 20 feet/turnMapping and careful observation of the surroundings are possible.
RunningBase movement rate times 40 feet/turnNo mapping permitted. Characters are automatically surprised and have no chance to surprise others. The sound of running may attract the attention of enemies.
CombatBase movement rate times 10 feet is how far the character can move in one round. Thus: Base movement of 6 = 60ft Base movement of 9 = 90ft Base movement of 12 = 120ft.Dashing around, battling your foes. Alternatively, running for your life. If some of the characters leave a combat (usually to circle around), they continue to move at the combat movement speed until they re-enter the combat area. This is actually faster than ordinary running speed, and incurs all the disadvantages of running.

INITIATIVE AND ORDER OF BATTLE — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Movement and Missile Fire: Each combatant on the side that won initiative may move, or may fire any missile weapons. Then the losers of the initiative roll may move or fire missile weapons. — 5. Melee Combat and Spells: Each combatant on the side that won initiative makes any melee attacks (if in melee range) or casts spells. Spells take effect immediately. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells.

6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished. — 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

INITIATIVE AND COMBAT ROUNDS — Combat rounds are five minutes long if troops are grouped into five-man units, and 10 minutes long if they are grouped into 10-man units. At the beginning of the battle, roll for initiative. Whichever side wins the initiative can choose to move first or last, and can also choose whether to attack first or last. For example, at the beginning of the Battle of Azure Wood, where the forces of Garfinkel the Wizard are opposing an invading goblin army, if Garfinkel wins the initiative he might choose to move first and attack first, to move first but attack last, to move and attack last, or to move last but attack first. All units on one side move or attack at the same time, and then those on the other side.

question: What can a player character do when they act (actions, movement, reactions/interrupts, free actions), and what limits apply?
answer: Combat actions are defined by the round procedure and its phases. In the standard combat sequence, each round follows an order of battle: check surprise (if needed), declare spells, roll initiative, then resolve movement and missile fire for the winning side and then the losing side, and finally resolve melee attacks and spellcasting for the winning side and then the losing side before completing the round and repeating. Within this structure, a combatant can take movement (including movement-like actions such as climbing onto tables or swinging from ropes) or fire missile weapons during the movement/missiles phase; and can make melee attacks (if in melee range) or cast spells during the melee/spells phase. Spellcasting has explicit limits: a spell must be declared before the initiative roll, and spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies; if the enemy wins initiative and damages the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. The book also presents alternate combat sequences: Method 1 keeps a three-phase round where the initiative winner may move and attack or may hold initiative to act later (the initiative loser cannot hold); Method 2 resolves actions in dexterity order with spells first, then missiles, then melee, then movement; Method 3 uses a segmented round with action intervals modified by armor, spell level, dexterity, and other factors, allowing faster characters to act more often.
expected_answer_summary: Combat options are phase-structured: move or fire missiles, then melee or cast spells, with limits like declaring spells before initiative and no spellcasting in melee; alternate sequences add holding initiative (Method 1), dex-order phases (Method 2), or segmented action timing (Method 3).

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
(This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)

Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells.

6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished. — 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

2. DECLARE SPELLS — Any player whose character is going to cast a spell must say so before the initiative roll. (The Referee makes a similar determination for the opponents.) The casting of any spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Thus, if the enemies win the initiative roll and damage the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. (This is the Swords & Wizardry interpretation of several places in the Original Rules where a spell must be “ready.”)

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
gold_chunks.text: Alternate Combat Sequence Method No. 2 — (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”) — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Surprise and Distance. The Referee determines if one side is surprised. As with all of the Referee’s decisions, this can be based on common sense instead of a die roll. The Referee may also determine the distance of the encounter (see above) if desired. This is exactly the same as in Method 1. — 2. Referee Determines Dexterity of Monsters. Instead of using several initiative rolls throughout the battle, Method 2 is based on dexterity scores. At this point, if the Referee hasn’t already noted the dexterity score of the monsters, now is the time to determine the dexterity scores of the monsters. There are many ways to do this, including (1) rolling 3d6 for each monster (not recommended because it’s slow), (2) deciding ahead of time that all members of a particular monster type always have the same dexterity, (3) rolling once for each monster type, or (4) rolling for all the monsters as a single group. The way the author usually does it is to roll once for each type of monster in the battle. So, for example, if there are 10 orcs and a single ogre on the other side, roll 3d6 once for the orcs (who thus all act at the same time) and 3d6 for the ogre (who thus acts at a different time than the orcs). — 3. Readied Spells are Cast and Take Effect. This is very different from the way spells are handled in Method 1 (see the Sidebar). All spells are cast in order of dexterity, from highest to lowest, including monster spellcasters. It is possible, for example, that at the beginning of a combat round, Barzoom the Mystical (a character with dexterity 18) casts his spell first, followed by the enemy dragon (dexterity 17), followed by Simoon the Slow (a character with dexterity 16). Barzoom was faster than the dragon, but the dragon was faster than Simoon.

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
gold_chunks.text:
4. Missile Attacks. Missiles may be fired in order of dexterity, and immediately inflict damage if they hit. See “The Attack Roll” below.
5. Combat. Melee (hand-to-hand) combat is resolved, with each opponent making attacks in order of dexterity. See “The Attack Roll” below.

question: What information must be tracked to play correctly (character stats/resources/conditions, positioning, clocks/time), and where is it recorded (character sheet, tokens, track, notes)?
answer: The rules call out that you complete a character sheet by recording your character’s statistics and equipment, and then play at the table with dice. Attribute scores (rolled 3d6 each) have mechanical effects that you should record, including bonuses/penalties (e.g., missile to-hit and Armor Class adjustments from Dexterity; Strength modifiers and carrying capacity/weight allowances; Constitution modifiers to hit points per hit die; Charisma limits on special hirelings and morale adjustments; and XP bonuses tied to Prime Attributes/Charisma/Wisdom). Treasure and money are tracked as gold pieces and other coinage, and treasure weight is tracked for encumbrance: weight is listed in pounds, a normal equipment load is assumed, and coins/gems have a defined weight; total carried weight determines base movement rate. Time is tracked in explicit procedural units (turns and combat rounds) that drive movement rates and other procedures. Consumable exploration resources like light sources have explicit durations (e.g., torch duration and lantern oil duration), which must be tracked to maintain light. Combat and exploration positioning/ranges are tracked at least in terms of distances/movement rates (feet per turn/round underground; yards per round outdoors), and combat procedures rely on tracked values like Armor Class and hit points.
expected_answer_summary: Track character sheet stats and equipment; attribute-derived bonuses; treasure/coin and its weight for encumbrance and movement; procedural time units (turn/round); consumables like light duration; and combat-relevant values like AC/HP and distances/movement.

gold_chunks.text: GETTING STARTED — Most likely, you already know basically how this game works. One of you is the “Referee,” who runs the game world: The Referee designs the adventures, makes decisions and rolls dice for the monsters, describes what the characters see, and judges the effects of their actions. The rest of you are the “players,” each of you taking on the role of a “player character” who might be a powerful wizard, a hard-bitten warrior, or any other sort of fantasy avatar representing you in the game world. You do not need to use miniatures, although they can sometimes help clarify who was standing underneath the stone block when it fell, and other such important questions. — The first step is to complete a character sheet, recording your character’s statistics and equipment; the next step is to sit down around a table with some dice and start playing! Unless, of course, you are the Referee, in which case you will have to prepare the adventure first. You have the Referee Book to help you with your role!

question: If the rules don’t cover a situation, what are you supposed to do at the table?
answer: The Referee makes the call. The rules are guidelines, not a complete procedure for every possibility: by design, Swords & Wizardry does not try to cover every single possibility for what players will do. The three most important principles stated explicitly on this point are: the rules are just guidelines; there is not a rule for everything; and when in doubt, make a ruling.
expected_answer_summary: When rules don't cover it, the Referee makes a ruling; the book explicitly states: rules are guidelines, no rule for everything, when in doubt make a ruling.

structural_path: When in doubt, make a ruling.

required_gold_rationale.d7e4a5f5ebe79ffb2b39482b9382734940d54a25: Core passage: 'When in doubt, make a ruling' — explicitly answers the question.

structural_path: When in doubt, make a ruling.
gold_chunks.text: The three most important things to know about running a game of Swords & Wizardry are these: — The rules are just guidelines — There is not a rule for everything. — When in doubt, make a ruling. — If you are a beginner Referee and have never played a role-playing game before, it is a good idea to stick to “the rules” for a few gaming sessions, just to learn how the game works. We have provided enough of these basic rules to get you started without having to make too many difficult decisions. But once you are comfortable with the basic idea of a roleplaying game, you can start thinking about the optional rules, about creating house rules, and about adapting the rules. A whole world of fantasy is out there waiting for you. — This book gives you the basic resources for the two main tasks of the Referee: creating the adventure, and running the adventure. Creating the adventure is done ahead of time; it is the job of dreaming up an adventurous place or situation, creating a map, and deciding what dangerous things lurk there, just waiting for a party of adventurers to stumble across them. Running the adventure is what happens when your friends are assembled around the gaming table with dice and character sheets: It is your job to describe the starting point and then to keep the game moving once the adventuring begins. As you and your players become more familiar with the game and their characters start to go up in level, you may want to start creating some details about the world that surrounds these adventures — which is called your campaign world. The following part of the book contains basic advice about all these aspects of being a Referee.

As the Referee of a game of Swords & Wizardry, you do not have a character; instead, you are the player who is in charge of the game. You create the adventure, you run the monsters, and you are the one who decides the results of all the decisions made by the players for their characters. You don’t get to tell the players what the characters do — that is their part of the game — but you referee the results according to the rules, and you interpret what the rules mean. Compared to most other fantasy roleplaying games, the Swords & Wizardry rules require a lot of interpretation because — by design — they do not try to cover every single possibility for what the players will do. It takes a little bit of practice to referee a game smoothly, but once you get the hang of it, you will find that you can run a fast-paced game without getting caught up in every little detail.

question: Does the game give a default procedure for dividing treasure or money among the party?
answer: The book provides no explicit procedure for dividing treasure among the party. Treasure division is a player-driven table decision: deciding how to split loot is part of what players do for their characters, and the Referee does not dictate those choices. If a dispute arises, the Referee adjudicates as with any other uncovered situation. This absence is consistent with the book's explicit design philosophy: there is not a rule for everything, the rules are guidelines, and when in doubt the Referee makes a ruling.
expected_answer_summary: No explicit procedure given; treasure division is a player table decision. The Referee adjudicates disputes if needed, consistent with the book's 'no rule for everything' philosophy.

gold_chunks.text: ![](images/0.jpg) — MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — READING THE MONSTER DESCRIPTIONS — The following is a quick reference guide for how to read monster descriptions: — Hit Dice (HD) is the number of dice (d8) rolled to determine an individual creature’s hit points. If there is a plus or minus next to the number, you add or subtract that number from the total, only once. For example: for a creature with 4+1 hit dice, you would roll 4d8, and then add one more hit point. — Armor Class (AC) is explained in the rules for combat. If you are using the descending AC system (where a lower AC is better), disregard the numbers in brackets. If you are using the ascending system (where a high AC is better), use the numbers in brackets. — Attacks (Atk) includes the number of attacks the monster has, and the damage these attacks inflict if they hit. Monsters get a separate attack roll for each attack. Monster attack rolls are made on the Monster Attack Table (p. 38). Note: if you are using the “ascending AC system,” do not bother using the chart, because there is a quicker way: Roll to hit, add the monster’s hit dice, and if the result is equal to or greater than the character’s armor class, the attack hits. — Saving Throw (Save) means the target number (on a d20) the monster needs to meet or exceed in order to make a successful saving throw. — Morale (Morale) is an optional rule used to determine if monsters flee from combat or surrender. — Special (Special) is usually just a “flag” for the Referee, to remind him or her that the monster has some unusual ability or feature. If necessary, more explanation may be included in the monster description.

Move (Move) is the monster’s movement rate, and it is handled just as movement rates are handled for characters. — Percent in Lair (% in Lair) is used when a monster is encountered on a random table (such as the wilderness encounter tables). Roll 1d100, and if the result is equal to or lower than the number listed, the characters have stumbled upon the monster’s lair. For some monsters, the concept of a lair is largely meaningless (they don’t travel, don’t make a nest, or live on another plane of existence) and these might be either “0” or “100,” depending on what seems most appropriate. — Challenge Level (CL) is used to separate the monsters into “difficulty levels,” so when you are creating an adventure you have some guidelines about what the characters can handle and what they cannot. — XP (XP) tells how many experience points the adventuring party gains for killing the creature. In some cases, you may choose to award experience points for defeating a creature without killing it. Circumventing a monster by creative means, or capturing it to bring home, are two examples of when such an award might be made. — A short stat block after each monster description offers relevant details of the creature listed in a smaller form. It includes all of the information presented in the larger entry, including special attacks. These descriptions are often used in adventures to describe a monster quickly so you can keep playing without having to look them up.

![](images/0.jpg) — HOW TO PLAY — Once you have created a character, the Referee will describe the setting and what your character can see. The game might start in a rural peasant village, in a vast and teeming city spiked with towers and minarets, in a castle, in a tavern, or at the gates of an ancient tomb — that is up to the Referee. But from that point on, you describe what your character does. Going down stairs, attacking a dragon, talking to people on the street — all of these sorts of things are your decisions. The Referee tells you what happens as a result. Maybe those stairs lead down to a huge and echoing tomb, or perhaps that dragon attacks your character with a storm of fiery breath. These are the Referee’s decisions. The rules below are guidelines for how to handle certain events: combat, movement, healing, dying, and other important parts of the game. — Basically, you and the Referee work together, with the Referee handling the details of a dangerous fantasy world, and you deciding what your character does in it. The epic story of your character’s rise to greatness (or unfortunate death) is yours to create in the Referee’s world.

question: How does treasure translate into experience points (if at all)?
answer: Characters earn experience points (XP) for two things: killing monsters and accumulating treasure. Each monster has a set XP value listed in its statistics. For treasure, the conversion is explicit: each gold piece acquired earns one XP. The book notes this rewards clever play — outwitting or bypassing monsters to secure loot counts just as much as combat.
expected_answer_summary: 1 gp = 1 XP; XP is also awarded for killing monsters (per listed monster value). Both treasure and monster kills are explicit XP sources.

gold_chunks.text: GAINING EXPERIENCE — Characters are awarded experience points (XP) for killing monsters and accumulating treasure. Monsters each have a set experience point value (given in the monster’s statistics), and each gold piece acquired earns one point as well. It may seem odd to award experience for treasure, but keep in mind that Swords & Wizardry is not just about slaying monsters — it is about outwitting your foes when you can! In fact, sometimes trickery and guile are the only way to handle a powerful monster such as a dragon or a demon. Skilled players avoid risking their characters’ lives if there is another, smarter way to walk out of the dungeon with a backpack full of gems and loot. — XP Bonus: Each Character Class has one or more Prime Attributes listed in its class description. If a character has this Prime Attribute at 13 or higher, all experience point awards to the character are increased by 5%. (If the class has more than one Prime Attribute, all must be 13 or above to earn to bonus.) Additionally, if the character has a Charisma score of 13 or more, experience point awards are increased by 5%. Finally, if the character has a Wisdom score of 13+, the character gains a third 5% bonus. Thus high Wisdom or Charisma may earn bonuses twice for a character, as the Prime Attribute as well as individually. It is possible for a character to gain as many as three 5% bonuses to experience point awards for a total increase of 15%. — Some multi-classed characters do not receive this bonus; see the rules for multi-classed characters for details. — When your character accumulates enough experience points to reach a new level, you roll another hit die and add that number to the character’s hit points. He or she may gain new spells or other abilities, as applicable, and combat skills may also increase. In other words, your character has become more powerful and can now pursue greater challenges!

question: Where do you find the XP thresholds for leveling up, and are they class-specific?
answer: XP thresholds for leveling up are class-specific. Each class has its own advancement table located on its class page: Assassin (p.9), Cleric (p.11), Druid (p.14), Fighter (p.15), Magic-User (p.17), Monk (p.19), Paladin (p.21), Ranger (p.23), and Thief (p.25). There is no single unified advancement table; each class defines its own XP thresholds independently. For multi-classed characters, XP earned is divided evenly among all classes regardless of attribute bonuses, and a new hit die is only gained when the character has advanced a level in all classes.
expected_answer_summary: Class-specific tables on each class's page (Assassin p.9, Cleric p.11, Druid p.14, Fighter p.15, Magic-User p.17, Monk p.19, Paladin p.21, Ranger p.23, Thief p.25); no single unified table.

gold_chunks.text: FIGHTER — You are a warrior trained in battle and in the use of armor and weapons. Perhaps you are a ferocious Viking raider, a roaming samurai, a dashing swashbuckler, a deadly swordswoman, or a chivalrous knight. Whatever type of Fighter you choose to play, you will probably end up on the front lines of your adventuring party — going toe-to-toe with dragons, goblins, and evil cultists, hacking your way through them and taking the brunt of their attacks. The Fighter character is the best-equipped of all the character classes to dish out damage and absorb it, too. Clerics heal and Magic-Users cast spells, but the down-and-dirty, hack-and-slash work is generally up to you. You are going to serve as the party’s sword and shield, protecting the weaker party members and taking down the enemies before you. Perhaps one day they will tell legends of your battle prowess, and followers will flock to your castle stronghold where you revel in your fame, riches, and newly-earned nobility. Fail, of course, and you will die, just another forgotten warrior in a dangerous world. It should go without saying that female Fighters are as deadly and as skillful as their male counterparts — and in the fantasy worlds of Swords & Wizardry, they are also much more common than in the history of our own world.

EXPERIENCE POINTS (XP) — Any experience points received are divided evenly among all the classes of a multi-classed character, even when the character can no longer advance in one or more classes. Multi-classed characters do not receive bonuses to experience points, regardless of their Attribute scores.

question: How does initiative work and what is the basic combat sequence for resolving a round?
answer: Each combat round follows a fixed 6-step sequence: (1) Check for Surprise — the Referee determines whether a surprise roll is needed; each side rolls 1d6 and is surprised on a 1 or 2 (Monk/Ranger parties are surprised only on a 1); one roll per side, not per individual. (2) Declare Spells — any player intending to cast must declare before the initiative roll; a surprised side skips this step and waits until the next round. (3) Determine Initiative — each side rolls 1d6; highest result wins; on a tie the Referee may treat actions as simultaneous or have both sides re-roll. (4) Movement and Missile Fire — initiative winners move or fire first; then losers do the same; winners cannot hold to see what foes do. (5) Melee Combat and Spells — initiative winners attack in melee and cast spells first; losers follow; creatures killed by winners are already dead and cannot act; Turning Undead also occurs here. (6) Round Complete — check ongoing effects, then return to step 2 and repeat until combat ends (one side flees, surrenders, or is entirely slain). Spells declared in step 2 may be lost if the caster takes damage before the melee/spells phase resolves. The book also presents alternate combat sequences (Method No. 1–3) that change the initiative/action ordering (including holding initiative, dexterity-ordered casting/attacks, and segmented action intervals), but the standard 6-step order above is the basic procedure.
expected_answer_summary: 6-step round: Surprise check, Declare Spells, Roll initiative (d6 per side, high wins), Movement/Missiles (winners first), Melee/Spells (winners first), then repeat from step 2.

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
(This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)

gold_chunks.text: INITIATIVE AND ORDER OF BATTLE — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Check for Surprise: The Referee determines if a surprise roll is required, and if so, both sides roll to see if they are surprised by the sudden encounter. See below for details of the surprise roll. — 2. Declare Spells: This is not an option for a group that was surprised; they have to wait until the beginning of the next round. See below for details of declaring spells. — 3. Determine Initiative: Each side rolls a d6 and the side with the highest result is the winner. One roll is made for each side, not for each individual. If this is the first round and one group was surprised, the other side gets to go first automatically, without having to roll a die. — 4. Movement and Missile Fire: Each combatant on the side that won initiative may move, or may fire any missile weapons. Then the losers of the initiative roll may move or fire missile weapons. — 5. Melee Combat and Spells: Each combatant on the side that won initiative makes any melee attacks (if in melee range) or casts spells. Spells take effect immediately. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat with enemies. Losers of the initiative then make their attacks and cast spells.

6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished. — 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

2. DECLARE SPELLS — Any player whose character is going to cast a spell must say so before the initiative roll. (The Referee makes a similar determination for the opponents.) The casting of any spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Thus, if the enemies win the initiative roll and damage the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. (This is the Swords & Wizardry interpretation of several places in the Original Rules where a spell must be “ready.”)

Take note: Monsters do not always leap to the attack. Unless they are on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine whether the monsters attack if they surprise the adventurers. On a roll of 2–6, the monsters are hostile and attack; on a roll of 7–9, the monsters withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; on a roll of 10–12, the monsters have a positive initial reaction to seeing the adventurers and might even be convinced to offer some help — if they are well paid, of course!

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
gold_chunks.text: Alternate Combat Sequence Method No. 2 — (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”) — When the party of adventurers comes into contact with enemies, the order of events is as follows: — 1. Surprise and Distance. The Referee determines if one side is surprised. As with all of the Referee’s decisions, this can be based on common sense instead of a die roll. The Referee may also determine the distance of the encounter (see above) if desired. This is exactly the same as in Method 1. — 2. Referee Determines Dexterity of Monsters. Instead of using several initiative rolls throughout the battle, Method 2 is based on dexterity scores. At this point, if the Referee hasn’t already noted the dexterity score of the monsters, now is the time to determine the dexterity scores of the monsters. There are many ways to do this, including (1) rolling 3d6 for each monster (not recommended because it’s slow), (2) deciding ahead of time that all members of a particular monster type always have the same dexterity, (3) rolling once for each monster type, or (4) rolling for all the monsters as a single group. The way the author usually does it is to roll once for each type of monster in the battle. So, for example, if there are 10 orcs and a single ogre on the other side, roll 3d6 once for the orcs (who thus all act at the same time) and 3d6 for the ogre (who thus acts at a different time than the orcs). — 3. Readied Spells are Cast and Take Effect. This is very different from the way spells are handled in Method 1 (see the Sidebar). All spells are cast in order of dexterity, from highest to lowest, including monster spellcasters. It is possible, for example, that at the beginning of a combat round, Barzoom the Mystical (a character with dexterity 18) casts his spell first, followed by the enemy dragon (dexterity 17), followed by Simoon the Slow (a character with dexterity 16). Barzoom was faster than the dragon, but the dragon was faster than Simoon.

structural_path: (This system comes from the 1978 “Holmes Blue Book Set”)
gold_chunks.text:
4. Missile Attacks. Missiles may be fired in order of dexterity, and immediately inflict damage if they hit. See “The Attack Roll” below.
5. Combat. Melee (hand-to-hand) combat is resolved, with each opponent making attacks in order of dexterity. See “The Attack Roll” below.

question: How often can a magic-user prepare spells, and what happens if they take damage while preparing/casting?
answer: Magic-Users prepare spells by reading from their spellbook and forcing selected spell formulae into their minds, preparing as many spells as they can mentally sustain; a spell can be prepared multiple times using available memory slots. Once a prepared spell is cast, it disappears from the Magic-User’s ability to cast until it is prepared again. The text does not state a fixed daily cadence for Magic-User preparation (unlike Clerics and Druids, who explicitly select and pray for a set of spells each day). If a spellcaster suffers damage while casting, the spell is lost; in the standard combat procedure, spells are declared before the initiative roll and casting starts at the beginning of the round, so if the enemy wins initiative and damages the spellcaster, the casting may be disturbed and the spell lost.
expected_answer_summary: Magic-Users prepare from a spellbook into memory slots (can prepare a spell multiple times); after casting it must be prepared again. If damaged while casting, the spell is lost; in combat, declaring happens before initiative and damage before resolution can lose the spell.

gold_chunks.text: SECOND RANK — Spears and polearms in the second rank of a battle formation can attack by reaching through the first rank. — SPELLS — Casting a spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Spells cannot be cast if the caster is in melee combat, although the Referee might rule that even if the caster is within 10 feet of an opponent, if blows have not been traded, then a spell can be cast. Wands and staffs are not subject to the restriction at all; they can cast their magic during melee combat. If the spellcaster suffers damage while casting a spell, the spell is lost. When the spell actually takes effect depends on which Combat Sequence Method is being used.

2. DECLARE SPELLS — Any player whose character is going to cast a spell must say so before the initiative roll. (The Referee makes a similar determination for the opponents.) The casting of any spell starts at the beginning of the combat round. Thus, if the enemies win the initiative roll and damage the spellcaster, the spell’s casting may be disturbed and the spell lost. (This is the Swords & Wizardry interpretation of several places in the Original Rules where a spell must be “ready.”)

question: On the Turn Undead table, what does a “T” result mean compared to a number?
answer: On the Turning the Undead table, a number is the minimum 2d10 result needed: if the roll is equal to or greater than that number, 2d6 of the targeted undead type are turned and depart (not returning for 3d6 rounds). If the table shows “T”, then 2d6 of the targeted undead are automatically turned and depart for 3d6 rounds (no roll threshold). If the table shows “D”, then 2d6 of the targeted undead are automatically destroyed and crumble to dust.
expected_answer_summary: Number = minimum 2d10 needed to turn; “T” = automatically turned; “D” = automatically destroyed (each affects 2d6 undead).

gold_chunks.text: TURNING THE UNDEAD — Lawful Clerics have the ability to “turn” the undead, causing the foul creatures to flee or even destroying them outright. When a Lawful Cleric attempts a turning, the player should roll 2d10 and consult the Turning the Undead table for the result.

Turning the Undead — Roll 2d10 to determine the result: —
Challenge Level of UndeadSpecific UndeadClerical Level
12345678910-1314-1718+
1Skeleton1074TTDDDDDDD
2Zombie131074TTDDDDD
3Ghoul16131074TTDD
4Shadow*1916131074TTDDDD
5201916131074
6Wight—201916131074TTDD
7Mummy——2019161310
8Wraith———201916131074TT
9Spectre————201916
10Vampire**—————201916131074
11Vampire**——————2019161310

1613

Shadows might not be undead creatures in your campaign; even so, they might be subject to being turned, just as demons (also not undead) can be turned. — ** Vampires and more-powerful undead cannot be turned automatically. — No Neutral Clerics? — The Cleric, as an armored warrior of the faith, using blunt weapons and having healing spells, is specific to the Lawful and Chaotic alignments. The Neutral equivalent of the Cleric is the Druid, worshipping an abstract power rather than gods. In fact, it is possible that there simply are no Neutrally-aligned gods; godlike power stems from Law or Chaos. The Cleric-like power of Druids comes from nature, an entirely different source. This is, of course, up to the Referee, who is the creator of the campaign world and all that is in it. — One solution, for the Referee who wants Neutral gods to grant Cleric-like power to their followers, is simply to allow Neutrally-aligned Clerics, who might or might not be able to turn the Undead (probably not). Another solution, if the Referee is feeling particularly energetic, would be to create a new character class, like the Druid, that serves Neutrally-aligned gods — an alternative counterpart to the Cleric class.

question: Does a 1st-level cleric get to cast spells?
answer: No. The Cleric Advancement Table shows no spells available at 1st level (all spell-level columns are blank). Clerics begin spellcasting at 2nd level, when they gain 1 first-level spell.
expected_answer_summary: No; 1st-level clerics have no spells. Spellcasting starts at 2nd level (1 first-level spell).

gold_chunks.text:
Cleric Advancement Table —
LevelXP Required for LevelHit Dice (d6)*Saving ThrowNumber of Spells (by level)***
1234567
10115———————
21,5002141———————
33,0003132—————
46,00041221—————
512,00051122—————

Hit points shown for levels after the character no longer gains full hit dice are the total combined number. An 11th level Cleric has 9HD plus 2 hit points total, not 9HD plus one hit point gained at 10th level and another 2 hit points gained at 11th.

question: What happens at 0 hit points, and when does a character die from damage?
answer: When damage reduces a character (or creature) to 0 hit points, the character is unconscious. If hit points are brought down to –1 or lower, the character dies. The book also describes a potential house rule: a character remains alive (bleeding at 1 hp/round without assistance) until reaching negative hit points equal to the character’s level (e.g., a 5th-level character dies at –5). For healing, natural recovery is 1 hit point per 2 days of uninterrupted rest (many Referees accelerate this to 1/day), and four weeks of rest returns a character to full hit points regardless of how many were lost.
expected_answer_summary: 0 HP = unconscious; –1 HP or lower = dead. Natural healing is 1 HP per 2 days of uninterrupted rest; four weeks restores to full.

gold_chunks.text: DAMAGE AND DEATH — When a character (or creature) is hit, the amount of damage is deducted from hit points. When total hit points reach 0, the character is unconscious, and if hit points are brought down to -1 or lower, the character dies. — A good potential house rule is attributed to Gary Gygax’s gaming table, a rumor that might or might not be true. It allows a character to remain alive (although bleeding to death at the rate of 1 hp/round if no assistance is rendered) until the character reaches negative hit points equal to the character’s level. In other words, a 5th-level character actually dies only upon reaching -5 hit points.

question: What happens if you fire a missile weapon into a melee where allies and enemies are mixed together?
answer: When attacking into a melee with missile weapons, it is usually not possible to choose which participant (opponent or friend) is hit. Instead, the Referee determines the actual target randomly.
expected_answer_summary: Missile fire into melee usually cannot pick a specific target; the Referee randomizes who is hit.

gold_chunks.text: MISSILE ATTACKS — Missile attacks are attacks with a ranged weapon such as a bow, crossbow, sling, or thrown axe. A character’s dexterity bonus for missile attacks is added to the to-hit roll when the character is using missile weapons, and Fighters may receive an extra bonus for strength. When using missiles to attack into a melee, it usually is not possible to choose which participant (opponent or friend) will receive the attack; the Referee determines this randomly.

question: How do saving throws work (what categories exist, what do you roll, and what number do you need)?
answer: In Swords & Wizardry, a saving throw is a d20 roll to avoid or lessen a hazard’s effect. Each class has a saving throw target number that gets lower (easier) as the character gains levels. To make a saving throw, roll 1d20; if the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number, the saving throw succeeds. Some classes have bonuses against particular hazards (e.g., Magic-Users get +2 on saving throws against spells), and these bonuses are described in the class write-ups. Multi-classed characters use the best available saving throw option from their classes, including any class bonuses, but you do not combine one class’s target number with another class’s bonus. Monsters also make saving throws: a monster’s saving throw target number may be listed in its description, and the book provides a monster saving throw table keyed by Hit Dice. The book also describes an alternative (Original Game) method with multiple saving-throw categories, but notes it does not integrate directly without house-ruling changes.
expected_answer_summary: Roll d20; succeed on result ≥ saving throw target number (class-based, improves with level; bonuses by class). Multi-class uses best option (don’t mix bonus/target). Monsters use listed target or HD table; an alternative multi-category save method is provided but is a house-rule variant.

gold_chunks.text:
SAVING THROWS — From time to time, a spell or some other kind of hazard requires you to make a “saving throw.” A successful saving throw means that the character (or monster) avoids the threat or lessens its effect. Each character class has a “Saving Throw” target number, which gets lower and lower — easier to beat, in other words — as a character gains levels. To make a saving throw, roll a d20. If the result is equal to or higher than the character’s saving throw target number, the saving throw succeeds.
Take note: Some character classes have better-than-average chances to make a saving throw against particular types of hazards. Magic-Users, for example, get a bonus of +2 to the die roll on their saving throws against spells of all kinds. These various bonuses are described in the explanation of each character class. Fighters, Paladins, and Rangers do not get these bonuses because their base saving throw numbers are better than the other classes across the board.

SAVING THROWS — The saving throw of a multi-classed character is the best available from the different choices, including any bonuses from a character class. For example, an elven Fighter/Magic-User of level 1/1 making a saving throw against a spell would compare the saving throw of a Fighter to the saving throw of a Magic-User. The Fighter’s saving throw would require a roll of 15. The Magic-User’s roll would require a roll of 15, but Magic-Users have a +2 saving throw bonus against spells, so as a Magic-User the Elf would require a roll of only 13 (since 13 + 2 is 15, the saving throw’s minimum number). Thus, the saving throw would be made as a Magic-User, requiring a minimum roll of 13 to succeed. The character would not use the Fighter’s saving throw (15) with the Magic-User’s bonus of +2; it must be one way or the other.

Monsters can also make saving throws; a monster’s saving throw target number is listed in its description. — Different Categories of Saving Throws — The Swords & Wizardry saving throw system is an adaptation of the original, which had several categories of different risks instead of a single basic saving throw as used here. The original version had the following categories and target numbers:

question: How do reaction rolls and morale checks work (when to roll, what dice, and how to interpret results)?
answer: Reaction: monsters do not always attack on sight. In particular, if monsters surprise the adventurers and are not on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine their initial reaction: 2–6 = hostile and attack; 7–9 = withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; 10–12 = positive initial reaction and may even offer help (possibly for pay). Morale: most monsters will not fight a hopeless battle; they may retreat, flee, or surrender. The Referee decides when monsters abandon the battle based on the situation and the monsters’ intelligence, and NPC allies may also flee if survival looks grim. The book points to an optional morale system elsewhere.
expected_answer_summary: Reaction can be a 2d6 roll (2–6 hostile attack; 7–9 wait; 10–12 positive) in the surprise/alertness context. Morale is primarily Referee-adjudicated unless using the optional morale system.

gold_chunks.text: 6. The round is complete. Go back to step 2 (Declare Spells) and repeat the process until the combat is finished. — 1. SURPRISE — In general, a group cannot be surprised if they are already aware of an opponent’s presence even though they have not yet seen them — due to hearing them on the other side of a door, for instance. — If one side is surprised, the other side automatically gets to take actions before the first initiative roll. If they also win that initiative roll, this means they will have taken two actions before the other side has a chance to do anything. If surprise is a possibility, roll 1d6 for either or both groups, as appropriate. (Roll only once for each side, not for each individual character or monster.) If the result is a 1 or a 2, the group is surprised. A party that includes a Monk or Ranger, however, is surprised only on a roll of 1. It is possible for both groups to be surprised, effectively canceling out the effect on both sides. — And here is something really nasty: If a group is surprised, each person (or creature) in it has a 1-in-4 chance to drop one or more handheld items! — Underground or in a dungeon, the distance between two groups when they discover each other is 1d6 x 10 feet. That is very close; the monsters may already be inside the radius of the adventurers' light source. Outside, the distance will be about 10 times this number, or, if the visibility is bad, three times. Anything affecting visibility could also affect surprise distance. For instance, someone with darkvision will be able to see up to 60 feet clearly at night or underground. A party carrying a light will be visible from much farther away, raising the possibility of an ambush. Other factors that may affect surprise are related to terrain types — thick woods or crowded city streets may decrease visibility and make it easier to surprise travelers. These and similar factors are left up to the judgment of the Referee.

Take note: Monsters do not always leap to the attack. Unless they are on the alert for intruders, the Referee may roll 2d6 to determine whether the monsters attack if they surprise the adventurers. On a roll of 2–6, the monsters are hostile and attack; on a roll of 7–9, the monsters withhold judgment and wait to see if the adventurers are hostile; on a roll of 10–12, the monsters have a positive initial reaction to seeing the adventurers and might even be convinced to offer some help — if they are well paid, of course!

MORALE — Certain monsters, such as mindless or undead creatures, are fearless and always fight to the death. The majority, however, will not continue to fight a hopeless battle; they will seek to retreat, flee, or surrender. The Referee decides when monsters abandon the battle and retreat, based on the situation and the monsters' intelligence. Keep in mind that the party’s own non-player character allies might decide to flee if their prospects of survival look grim. See page 39 for an optional system to handle morale. — ![](images/0.jpg) — Level 1 — 1. Charm Person — 2. Detect Magic — 3. Hold Portal — 4. Light — 5. Protection from Chaos — 6. Read Languages — 7.
